problem.title: Longest Increasing Path in a Matrix
problem.description: Given an m×n integer matrix, return the length of the longest increasing path. From each cell you may move in 4 directions (up, down, left, right) to adjacent cells with strictly greater values. You may not move diagonally or outside the boundary.
assumptions: m and n are at least 1.

example.explanation: The longest increasing path is [1, 2, 6, 9], length 4.

solutions.title: Solution 1 – DFS + Memoization
solutions.description: For each cell, perform a DFS that explores all 4 neighbors with strictly greater values and returns the maximum path length starting from that cell. Memoize each cell's result so repeated subproblems are resolved in O(1). Because the path must be strictly increasing, there are no cycles and the recursion is always well-founded, allowing the cache to be filled correctly.

complexity.time: O(mn) — each cell computed once and cached
complexity.space: O(mn) for the memoization table and recursion stack

example_walkthrough.text: For matrix [[9,9,4],[6,6,8],[2,1,1]], starting at cell (2,1) with value 1: neighbors with greater values are (2,0)=2 and (1,1)=6. DFS(2,0)=2 extends to (1,0)=6 → (0,0)=9, giving length 3. DFS(2,1) therefore returns max(1+3, 1+dfs(1,1))=4. The answer is 4.
